records.ety: [OF. despeechier, F. dpcher; prob. from pref. des- (L. dis-) + (assumed) LL. pedicare to place obstacles in the way, fr. L. pedica fetter, fr. pes, pedis, foot. See Foot, and cf. Impeach, Despatch.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Dispatched (?); p. pr. & vb. n. Dispatching.]

defs: To dispose of speedily, as business; to execute quickly; to make a speedy end of; to finish; to perform.
quotes: Ere we put ourselves in arms, dispatch we The business we have talked of.
[The] harvest men . . . almost in one fair day dispatcheth all the harvest work.
I had clean dispatched myself of this great charge.

defs: To get rid of by sending off; to send away hastily.
quotes: Unless dispatched to the mansion house in the country . . . they perish among the lumber of garrets.

defs: To send off or away; -- particularly applied to sending off messengers, messages, letters, etc., on special business, and implying haste.
quotes: Even with the speediest expedition I will dispatch him to the emperor's cou<?/<?/.

defs: To send out of the world; to put to death.
quotes: The company shall stone them with stones, and dispatch them with their swords.

defs: To make haste; to conclude an affair; to finish a matter of business.

records.ety: [Cf. OF. despeche, F. dpche. See Dispatch, v. t.]

defs: The act of sending a message or messenger in haste or on important business.

quotes: To the utter dispatch of all their most beloved comforts.

defs: The finishing up of a business; speedy performance, as of business; prompt execution; diligence; haste.
quotes: To carry his scythe . . . with a sufficient dispatch through a sufficient space.

defs: A message dispatched or sent with speed; especially, an important official letter sent from one public officer to another; -- often used in the plural; as, a messenger has arrived with dispatches for the American minister; naval or military dispatches.
